title: Formerly CODEX STAN C-1-1966. Adopted in 1996. Revised in 2007.

sections.section: Formerly CODEX STAN C-1-1966. Adopted in 1996. Revised in 2007.
sections.summary: Document history and title information noting the standard was formerly CODEX STAN C-1-1966, adopted in 1996, revised in 2007, and that the Standard for Cheddar (CXS 263-1966) has been amended in listed years; not substantive regulatory requirements.

sections.section: ## 2022 Amendment The following amendment was made to the text of the standard following decisions taken at the forty-fifth session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.
sections.summary: Records a 2022 amendment replacing the previous detailed labelling requirements for non-retail containers (Section 7.5) with a requirement that such labelling be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Scope: this Standard applies to Cheddar intended for direct consumption or for further processing as described in Section 2.

Describes Cheddar as a ripened hard cheese with near white to light yellow/orange colour, firm smooth waxy texture, generally no gas holes and optional rind or coating; ripening to develop flavour/body is normally about 5 weeks at 7–15 °C though alternative ripening methods (including ripening enzymes) are permitted if equivalent characteristics are achieved, and cheese intended for further processing may be less mature.

Sets out essential composition and quality factors: permitted raw materials (cow/buffalo milk and derived products), permitted ingredients (starters, coagulating enzymes, salts, water, ripening enzymes, processing aids and specified flours/starches as surface anti-caking agents for cut/sliced/shredded products), and compositional requirements including minimum milk fat in dry matter (22%) with reference fat-in-dry-matter bands and corresponding minimum dry matter levels; deviations beyond the specified minima/maxima are non-compliant with CXS 206-1999.
Permits raw materials limited to cows' milk, buffaloes' milk, their mixtures and products derived from these milks.

Lists permitted ingredients for Cheddar including harmless starter and other cultures, rennet/other coagulating enzymes, sodium or potassium chloride, potable water, safe ripening enzymes and processing aids, and allows rice/corn/potato flours/starches only as surface anti-caking agents for cut/sliced/shredded products in amounts functionally necessary under GMP.

Specifies compositional criteria: minimum milk fat in dry matter 22% (no upper limit), reference fat-in-dry-matter 48–60%, and minimum dry matter levels tied to fat-in-dry-matter bands (49%, 53%, 57%, 61%, 66% respectively for increasing bands); notes that compositional modifications beyond these minima/maxima are not compliant with CXS 206-1999.

Permits only additive functional classes justified in the accompanying table and in accordance with the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995); table indicates justified uses such as colours in cheese mass, acidity regulators and preservatives in cheese mass, preservatives on surface, and anti-caking agents for surface/rind treatment (with use limited to those listed in applicable GSFA tables).

Requires the products and the milk used in their manufacture to comply with Maximum Levels for contaminants and toxins in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with CAC-established maximum residue limits for veterinary drugs and pesticides in milk.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57-2004) and other relevant Codex hygienic texts, and that products comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.

States that, besides general labelling standards, specific provisions apply to Cheddar including the conditions for use of the name, origin declaration, milk fat declaration, date marking and non-retail labelling; these subsections set out when the name 'Cheddar' may be used, labelling obligations for origin and fat content, date marking allowances for non-final-consumer products, and reference to CXS 346-2021 for non-retail containers.

Permits use of the name 'Cheddar' only for cheese that conforms to this Standard (alternative spellings allowed where customary) and requires products with fat content outside the reference range but above the absolute minimum to carry a clear qualifier describing the modification or fat content; the name may also be used for cut, sliced, shredded or grated products made from conforming cheese.

Requires declaration of the country of origin (meaning country of manufacture), and when substantial transformation occurs in a second country that country is to be declared as the origin for labelling purposes.

Requires declaration of milk fat content in a manner acceptable in the country of retail sale, either as percentage by mass, percentage of fat in dry matter, or grams per serving (with number of servings stated).

Allows the date of manufacture to be declared instead of minimum durability information when the product is not intended for purchase by the final consumer; additionally, for comparative nutritional claims the reference minimum fat-in-dry-matter is 48%, and routine repackaging/cutting/slicing/shredding/grating are not considered substantial transformation.
Requires labelling of non-retail containers to be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
For compliance checks, directs use of the methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to provisions of this Standard.
